data.firstLineTreatment:
Lifestyle modifications (DASH diet, sodium reduction <1,500 mg/day, weight loss, aerobic exercise 150 min/week, limited alcohol). 
Pharmacotherapy for Stage 1 (>=130/80) with high ASCVD risk or Stage 2 (>=140/90): 
1. ACE inhibitors (e.g., Lisinopril 10-40 mg QD) 
2. ARBs (e.g., Losartan 50-100 mg QD) 
3. Thiazide-type diuretics (e.g., Chlorthalidone 12.5-25 mg QD) 
4. Calcium Channel Blockers (e.g., Amlodipine 5-10 mg QD).
data.alternatives: Beta-blockers (preferred in patients with HFrEF or CAD), Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (Spironolactone for resistant hypertension), Alpha-1 blockers, or Central alpha-2 agonists.
data.redFlags: Hypertensive crisis (BP >180/120 mmHg), Chest pain (acute coronary syndrome), Shortness of breath (pulmonary edema), Neurological deficits (stroke/TIA), Acute vision changes (retinal hemorrhage), Severe headache/confusion (encephalopathy).
data.followUp: Re-evaluate monthly until blood pressure goal (<130/80 mmHg) is achieved. Once controlled, follow up every 3 to 6 months. Annual labs: CMP (electrolytes/renal function), fasting glucose/A1C, lipid panel, and urinalysis.
data.references: AHA/ACC 2017 Guideline for High Blood Pressure in Adults

data.firstLineTreatment:
Lifestyle management: Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT), weight management (7% loss goal), and physical activity (150 min/week). 
Pharmacotherapy: Metformin (initial 500 mg QD, titrate to 1,000 mg BID) is the preferred initial agent if eGFR >30 mL/min. 
Early combination therapy may be considered if A1C is 1.5-2.0% above target.
data.alternatives: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists (e.g., Semaglutide) or SGLT2 Inhibitors (e.g., Empagliflozin) for patients with ASCVD, Heart Failure, or CKD regardless of A1C. DPP-4 inhibitors, Pioglitazone, or Sulfonylureas are secondary options. Insulin for severe hyperglycemia (A1C >10%).
data.redFlags: Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS) or DKA (confusion, extreme thirst, polyuria, fruity breath), Severe Hypoglycemia (glucose <54 mg/dL), Non-healing foot ulcers (risk of osteomyelitis/amputation), Sudden vision loss, Unexplained weight loss.
data.followUp: A1C every 3 months if not at goal; every 6 months if stable and meeting goals. Annual screenings: Comprehensive foot exam, Dilated eye exam, UACR (microalbuminuria), and Lipid panel. Target A1C <7.0% for most non-pregnant adults.
data.references: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes 2024

data.firstLineTreatment:
GINA 2024 recommends Track 1 (Preferred): Low-dose Inhaled Corticosteroid (ICS)-formoterol as needed for symptom relief (rescue) and as maintenance for Steps 3-5. 
Track 2: SABA rescue (e.g., Albuterol) PLUS regular daily low-dose ICS to reduce risk of severe exacerbations.
data.alternatives: Leukotriene Receptor Antagonists (LTRA, e.g., Montelukast), Long-Acting Muscarinic Antagonists (LAMA), Biologic therapies (e.g., Omalizumab) for severe eosinophilic asthma, Bronchial thermoplasty for refractory cases.
data.redFlags: Status asthmaticus: Silent chest (no air movement), Cyanosis (blue lips/nails), Inability to speak in full sentences, Peak expiratory flow <50% predicted, Accessory muscle use, Paradoxical pulse, Altered mental status.
data.followUp: Review 1-3 months after treatment initiation, then every 3-12 months. Assess symptom control (ACT score), medication technique/adherence, and lung function (Spirometry). After an exacerbation, follow up within 1 week.
data.references: GINA 2024 Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention

data.firstLineTreatment:
Pharmacotherapy: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs - e.g., Sertraline 50-200 mg, Escitalopram 10-20 mg) or SNRIs (e.g., Venlafaxine, Duloxetine). 
Psychotherapy: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) or Interpersonal Therapy (IPT). 
Combination therapy (Medication + Psychotherapy) is most effective for moderate to severe cases.
data.alternatives: Atypical antidepressants (e.g., Bupropion, Mirtazapine), Tricyclic Antidepressants (TCAs), MAOIs (rarely used). Augmentation with atypical antipsychotics or Lithium. Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) or Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) for treatment-resistant depression.
data.redFlags: Suicidal ideation with plan/intent, Psychosis (hallucinations/delusions), Catatonia, Severe self-neglect (dehydration/starvation), Serotonin Syndrome signs (if on meds), Bipolar switch (manic symptoms after starting antidepressants).
data.followUp: Initially every 1-2 weeks to monitor for suicidal ideation and side effects. Assess response at 4-6 weeks (PHQ-9 score). Continue treatment for at least 6-9 months after remission to prevent relapse. For recurrent episodes, long-term maintenance may be required.
data.references: WHO Mental Health Gap Action Programme (mhGAP); APA Practice Guideline

data.firstLineTreatment:
Psychotherapy: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) including exposure therapy. 
Pharmacotherapy: SSRIs (e.g., Paroxetine, Sertraline) or SNRIs (e.g., Duloxetine, Venlafaxine) are first-line for GAD, Panic Disorder, and Social Anxiety. Start at half-depression doses to minimize initial jitteriness.
data.alternatives: Buspirone (for GAD specifically), Pregabalin, Hydroxyzine. Benzodiazepines (e.g., Lorazepam, Alprazolam) only for short-term use/crisis management (risk of dependence). Beta-blockers (e.g., Propranolol) for performance anxiety/physical symptoms.
data.redFlags: Suicidal ideation, Co-occurring substance use disorder, Severe panic attacks mimicking cardiac events, Significant functional impairment (inability to leave home/work), Agoraphobia development, Paradoxical reaction to benzodiazepines.
data.followUp: Monitor every 2 weeks initially, then monthly until stable. Use GAD-7 scale to track progress. Treatment should continue for 6-12 months after symptoms stabilize. Taper medications slowly to avoid withdrawal/rebound anxiety.
data.references: WHO mhGAP Intervention Guide; NICE Clinical Guidelines

data.firstLineTreatment:
Heart-healthy lifestyle: Saturated fat <7% of calories, eliminate trans fats, high fiber (soluble), plant sterols, and 150 min/week exercise. 
Pharmacotherapy: HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors (Statins). High-intensity (Atorvastatin 40-80 mg, Rosuvastatin 20-40 mg) for clinical ASCVD or LDL >=190 mg/dL.
data.alternatives: Ezetimibe (initial add-on to statins), PCSK9 Inhibitors (Evolocumab) for very high risk, Bempedoic acid, Fibrates (primarily for hypertriglyceridemia >500 mg/dL to prevent pancreatitis), Omega-3 fatty acids (Vascepa).
data.redFlags: Statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS): Rhabdomyolysis (severe pain, dark tea-colored urine, weakness), LDL >=190 mg/dL in young adults (familial hypercholesterolemia), Acute pancreatitis (triglycerides >1,000 mg/dL).
data.followUp: Repeat lipid panel 4-12 weeks after starting or adjusting statin therapy to assess adherence and percentage LDL reduction. Once stable, check every 3-12 months. Monitor ALT/AST only if symptoms of hepatotoxicity occur.
data.references: AHA/ACC 2018 Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol

data.firstLineTreatment:
Supportive care (90% are viral): Rest, hydration, honey for cough (adults/children >1y), saline nasal drops/rinses. 
Symptomatic relief: NSAIDs (Ibuprofen) or Acetaminophen for fever/sore throat/headache. Oral decongestants (Pseudoephedrine) for adults (short-term use).
data.alternatives: Intranasal steroids for significant congestion. Antihistamines for rhinorrhea (older generation more effective but sedating). Dextromethorphan for non-productive cough. Ipratropium nasal spray for severe rhinorrhea.
data.redFlags: Bacterial complications: Persistent symptoms >10 days without improvement, 'Double worsening' (new fever/cough after initial recovery), High fever (>102F) with purulent nasal discharge for 3+ days (Sinusitis), Drooling/stridor (Epiglottitis), Neck stiffness (Meningitis).
data.followUp: Usually not required for uncomplicated URI. Advise 'watchful waiting' for 7-10 days. Instruct patient to return if symptoms worsen, respiratory distress develops, or fever persists beyond 3-5 days. Discourage routine antibiotic use for 'common cold'.
data.references: CDC Adult Symptomatic Treatment for Common Cold and URI

data.firstLineTreatment:
Uncomplicated Cystitis (Women): 
1. Nitrofurantoin (Macrobid) 100 mg BID for 5 days 
2. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim DS) 1 BID for 3 days (if local resistance <20%) 
3. Fosfomycin 3g single dose. 
Increase fluid intake.
data.alternatives: Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin/Levofloxacin) should be reserved for complicated cases or when first-line options cannot be used. Beta-lactams (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate, Cefdinir) for 3-7 days. Phenazopyridine for short-term symptomatic pain relief.
data.redFlags:
Pyelonephritis: Flank pain, Costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness, Fever/chills, Nausea/vomiting. 
Sepsis: Hypotension, Tachycardia, Confusion. 
Complicated UTI: Pregnancy, Male gender, Immunosuppression, Nephrolithiasis, Recent catheterization.
data.followUp: Not required for uncomplicated cystitis if symptoms resolve. For recurrent UTIs (>=3/year), consider lifestyle changes, post-coital prophylaxis, or vaginal estrogen in postmenopausal women. Culture/sensitivity required for all complicated or non-resolving cases.
data.references: IDSA Guidelines for Treatment of Uncomplicated Cystitis

data.firstLineTreatment:
Lifestyle: Weight loss, elevating head of bed, avoiding late-night meals (within 3 hours of sleep), tobacco/alcohol cessation, and avoiding trigger foods (caffeine, chocolate, spicy). 
Pharmacotherapy: Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs, e.g., Omeprazole 20 mg QD) for 8 weeks.
data.alternatives: H2 Receptor Antagonists (e.g., Famotidine) for mild/intermittent symptoms. Antacids (Tums, Maalox) for immediate relief. Baclofen or Prokinetics (rarely). Surgical options (Nissen fundoplication) or endoscopic therapies for refractory cases.
data.redFlags: Alarm symptoms: Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing), Odynophagia (painful swallowing), Unexplained weight loss, Iron deficiency anemia or GI bleeding (melena/hematemesis), Persistent vomiting, New onset symptoms in patients >60 years old.
data.followUp: Assess response after 8 weeks of PPI therapy. If successful, attempt to taper to the lowest effective dose or 'on-demand' H2RA therapy. Refer for Endoscopy (EGD) if alarm symptoms are present or if symptoms persist despite optimized PPI therapy.
data.references: ACG Clinical Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of GERD

data.firstLineTreatment:
Acute: NSAIDs (Ibuprofen 400-800 mg, Naproxen 500 mg) or Acetaminophen for mild-moderate attacks. 
Specific Acute: Triptans (e.g., Sumatriptan 50-100 mg) for moderate-severe attacks, taken at earliest onset. 
Preventive (>=4 days/month): Beta-blockers (Propranolol), Topiramate, Amitriptyline.
data.alternatives: CGRP Antagonists (e.g., Ubrogepant for acute; Erenumab for prevention), Gepants, Ditans (Lasmiditan). Magnesium, Riboflavin (B2), or Coenzyme Q10 for natural prevention. Botox injections for chronic migraine (>=15 days/month).
data.redFlags: SNOOP mnemonic: Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss), Neurologic signs, Onset sudden (Thunderclap headache), Older age at onset (>50y), Progression or change in pattern. Pregnancy (limited drug options). Aura lasting >60 minutes.
data.followUp: Maintain a headache diary to identify triggers and monitor frequency/severity. Re-evaluate every 2-4 weeks when initiating prevention. Screen for Medication Overuse Headache (MOH) if acute treatments used >10-15 days/month. Target 50% reduction in frequency.
